course_description:
The National Disability Insurance Scheme underpins this course which focuses on the management of disability associated with communication and/or swallowing difficulties in people under 65 years of age. The course addresses the provision of augmentative and alternative communication in people with severe communication impairments.

In this course, students learn to describe the characteristics associated with common disabilities across the lifespan using the ICF framework (WHO, 2001) and to incorporate the principles of Person- and Family-Centred Care (PFCC) as part of the holistic assessment and management of clients with disability, as well as family support and advocacy within the community.

course_aims:
This course focuses on the management of disability associated with communication and/or swallowing difficulties in people under 65 years of age. Through a combination of online learning resources and active participation in tutorials, students will further develop their skills in providing person- and family-centred care when working with clients/communities with a disability. Students will develop skills in applying the International Classification of Functioning, Disability and Health (2001) framework for the identification of appropriate assessments used to evaluate clients/communities with a disability. Through authentic case-based learning scenarios, students will develop skills in the integration and interpretation of assessment data and in the designing and implementation of evidence-based speech pathology intervention to address client/community goals for people with a disability.

Through completion of a Faculty of Health, Medicine and Behavioural Sciences online National Disability Insurance Scheme (NDIS) learning module, students will develop an understanding of NDIS legislation, the participant's journey through the NDIS, the types of supports they can access, and the role of interprofessional teams in supporting clients/communities with a disability. Guest lecturers and the practical demonstration of augmentative and alternative communication options for people with complex communication needs and their communication partners is included in the course.

This course offers students a flipped approach to learning. Students engage in online learning resources before weekly tutorials where they then participate in authentic learning experiences with peers and teaching staff. In the tutorials, students engage in peer-to-peer learning through active participation in independent, small group and large group inquiry-based learning activities. This provides students with more flexibility to learn, but the associated workload is similar to that of a

learning_outcomes.description: Engage critically and constructively with frameworks, legislation, philosophies of care and contemporary issues associated with disability to advocate in an everchanging landscape.
Describe and interpret appropriate assessments across the ICF framework to identify communication and swallowing support needs for people with a disability.
Design evidence-based speech pathology intervention to address person- and family-centred goals of people with disability.
Explain the application of augmentative and alternative communication options for people with complex communication needs and their communication partners.
Work in a professional team, including the provision of effective, and constructive feedback, to support communication and swallowing for people with disabilities.
Describe the key characteristics of disabilities that impact communication and swallowing across the lifespan.

assessment_details.title: Video Reflection
assessment_details.task_description: After attending the Lived Experience of Disability Panel, students are required to submit one (1) video reflection. The panel discussion will inform this assessment and requires students to critically reflect on the perspectives shared by the panellists. In this assessment, students are expected to move beyond a descriptive summary of the panel and instead integrate insights from the discussion with relevant course concepts related to disability across the lifespan, communication, and professional speech pathology practice. Students should also reflect on how this learning will influence their future clinical reasoning and practice. A detailed task description will be uploaded to the course Blackboard site. Artificial Intelligence (AI) and Machine Translation (MT) are emerging tools that may support students in completing this assessment task. Students may appropriately use AI and/or MT in completing this assessment task. Students must clearly reference any use of AI or MT in each instance. A failure to reference generative AI or MT use may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct. Read full task description Hide full task description
assessment_details.submission_guidelines:
The assessment task is to be submitted via the course e-learning Blackboard site.
Note. When a student submits an assignment or test, a window appears with a submission confirmation number. Students can download a copy of the receipt directly from the window. They also receive a copy of their submission receipt via an automatic email confirmation. Students are encouraged to save their submission receipts to verify they submitted their work.
Deferral or extension
You may be able to apply for an extension .
The maximum extension allowed is 14 days. Extensions are given in multiples of 24 hours.
Extensions can be requested for this assessment item. Please apply for an extension request via my.UQ > my.UQ Dashboard > my. Requests and provide evidence of circumstance, as soon as it becomes evident that an extension is needed, but no later than the assessment item submission date. Further information regarding what constitutes an acceptable reason for an extension is listed on the my.UQ website: Applying for an extension - my.UQ - University of Queensland .
Late submission
A penalty of 10% of the maximum possible mark will be deducted per 24 hours from time submission is due for up to 7 days. After 7 days, you will receive a mark of 0.

learning_resources.library_resources: Find the required and recommended resources for this course on the UQ Library website.

learning_activities.learning_period: Multiple weeks From Week 1 To Week 13 (23 Feb - 31 May)
learning_activities.topic: Online self-directed learning modules (self-directed learning) A series of weekly online modules will provide information on the holistic management of evidence-based practice for people with disability across the lifespan. The self-directed learning module content will cover a broad range of topics, including contemporary and evolving views of disability, the role of speech pathologists and interprofessional teams in supporting people with disability, supporting people with lifelong conditions (e.g., cerebral palsy, intellectual disability, cleft palate, hearing loss), and supporting clients with complex communication and swallowing disabilities (both assessment and intervention). The online modules will also cover Augmentative and Alternative Communication (AAC) for people with disability, including an overview of what AAC is and who benefits from it, AAC trial considerations and implementation, including the role of Key Word Sign (KWS).

learning_activities.learning_period: Multiple weeks From Week 1 To Week 13 (23 Feb - 31 May)
learning_activities.topic: Weekly on campus tutorials Tutorials will be conducted from weeks 1-13 of semester and will incorporate inquiry-based learning and small group discussion to further explore speech pathology approaches to the holistic and evidence-based management of people with disability across the lifespan. On campus tutorials will include practical sessions delivered by experts in Augmentative and Alternative Communication (AAC) and Key Word Sign (KWS) to support students' understanding of best practice when working with clients with disability. Tutorials have been designed for experiential learning; therefore, students are expected to attend and actively participate in all tutorials. It is essential that students prepare for tutorials by completing the online self-paced learning modules and associated preparation activities. As students will be working in small groups during this learning activity, tutorials will not be recorded.

learning_activities.learning_period: Multiple weeks From Week 1 To Week 13 (23 Feb - 31 May)
learning_activities.topic: Graduate Ready Module: ‘Skills for graduates working alongside the NDIS in practice' Given the impact of the National Disability Insurance Scheme (NDIS) on service provision, students must complete ‘graduate-ready’ modules which are designed to prepare them for working in the disability sector upon graduation. Content from these modules will be assessed in the course in-semester examination.
Introductory lecture The introductory lecture (week 1) will provide an overview of evidence-based practice for working with people with disabilities and their families across the lifespan.

Supporting Clients with Craniofacial Abnormalities Workshop This workshop will be facilitated by the Cleft Palate / Craniofacial Team, Speech Pathology Department, Queensland Children’s Hospital and will explore how speech pathologists support communication and feeding across the lifespan in people with craniofacial abnormalities. This workshop will be held on campus and will not be recorded. Note. This workshop is timetabled on a Friday and will replace the weekly tutorial in Week 7 for SPCH3111.

Lived Experience of Disability Lecture In this learning activity, you will explore disability through direct engagement with a person with disability and their treating professionals. The session is designed to centre lived experience and professional perspectives, providing insight into how disability is experienced, supported, and understood in everyday life and clinical practice. By attending this activity, you will have the opportunity to listen to personal stories, learn about the roles of different health professionals, and reflect on how collaboration, communication, and respect underpin person‑centred care. This interactive panel-lecture also provides a safe and respectful space for you to ask questions and engage in meaningful dialogue with the panellists. Note*: Due to the nature of this activity and the availability of the panellists, it will be scheduled on a Tuesday, outside typical tutorial hours.

Final Practical The final lecture (Week 13) will review the course as a whole, including assessment coaching for the AAC demonstration practical (due Week 13) and the case-based assessment (due Examination Week 1). Attendance in person is strongly encouraged.
